title: SEBI v. Kishore R. Ajmera

parties.petitioner: Securities and Exchange Board of India

legal_principles: Preponderance of evidence in SEBI proceedings
Circumstantial evidence sufficient for insider trading
summary: SEBI held trader guilty of insider trading based on circumstantial evidence — trading in shares of a company immediately before announcement of merger, having connection to persons in possession of unpublished price-sensitive information (UPSI). Supreme Court upheld SEBI's order and affirmed that circumstantial evidence and preponderance of probability standard applies in SEBI proceedings (not 'beyond reasonable doubt').
key_holdings: SEBI proceedings are quasi-judicial — preponderance of probability standard applies (not criminal 'beyond reasonable doubt')
Circumstantial evidence is sufficient to prove insider trading
Connections to UPSI holders combined with suspicious trading patterns establishes guilt
SEBI can impose civil penalties based on circumstantial chain of evidence
why_important: Clarified the evidentiary standard in SEBI proceedings. Confirmed SEBI can use circumstantial evidence to prove insider trading — significant for securities law enforcement.
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Critical for understanding SEBI compliance and insider trading laws applicable to listed companies.
